---
author: gp
date: 2026-06-17
subtitle: China built foundational software. India built services.
title: The Software Myth
---

```{=org}
#+STARTUP: overview indent
```
```{=org}
#+PROPERTY: header-args :exports both :eval no-export
```
```{=org}
#+PROPERTY: header-args:python :results output
```
The standard framing goes like this: China dominates hardware ---
semiconductors, manufacturing, supply chains --- while India dominates
software. It is repeated in boardrooms, policy papers, and technology
journalism with the confidence of settled fact. It is also wrong, in a
way that matters.

China has built a full-stack alternative to US enterprise software
infrastructure. Databases, operating systems, RPC frameworks, service
meshes, consensus protocols, cryptography libraries --- the layer that
other software runs on, built from scratch, open-sourced, and now
deployed commercially across Asia. India\'s \"software advantage\" is a
services advantage: Indian engineers writing code for Western companies
on Western infrastructure. That is valuable. It is not the same thing.

This distinction --- between foundational software and services --- is
not semantic. The country that controls foundational infrastructure
controls the terms on which everything above it is built. China
understood this earlier than most, built accordingly, and now has a
durable lead in open-source infrastructure software that is almost
entirely invisible in the standard China-versus-India technology
narrative.

# What Foundational Software Actually Means {#foundational}

Foundational software is not application code. It is not services. It is
the layer that, if it disappears, everything above it stops working.
Databases. Operating systems. Network proxies. Message queues. RPC
frameworks. Consensus algorithms. Cryptography libraries. The software
that sits below your application and above your hardware.

The table below maps verifiable OSS contributions by layer. Open
protocols (UPI, Aadhaar) are listed separately --- they are significant
but are specifications, not deployable code.

  Layer                China                                        India
  -------------------- -------------------------------------------- --------------------
  OS / Kernel          openEuler (Huawei), Asterinas (Ant)          ---
  Virtualization       Kata Containers (Hyper.sh, partial)          ---
  Database             OceanBase, TiDB, openGauss, Doris, TuGraph   ---
  Messaging            RocketMQ (Alibaba)                           ---
  RPC / Networking     Dubbo, SOFARPC, Kitex (ByteDance), BFE       ---
  Service Mesh         MOSN (Ant)                                   ---
  Service Registry     Nacos (Alibaba), Polaris (Tencent)           ---
  Distributed Txn      Seata (Alibaba)                              ---
  Consensus            SOFAJRaft (Ant)                              ---
  Cryptography         Tongsuo (Ant)                                ---
  ML Framework         PaddlePaddle (Baidu), MindSpore (Huawei)     ---
  Container Registry   Harbor (VMware Shanghai)                     ---
  API Gateway          APISIX (API7)                                ---
  Git Hosting          Gitea (Chinese-origin)                       ---
  App Framework        SOFABoot (Ant)                               Frappe (Mumbai)
  Identity Platform    ---                                          MOSIP (IIIT-B)
  *Open Protocol*      ---                                          UPI, Aadhaar, ONDC

The global foundational software stack was built almost entirely in the
United States: Linux, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Kafka, gRPC, Envoy, etcd,
TensorFlow. India contributed engineers to many of these projects ---
individually, as employees of US companies. India as a country, through
its own companies and institutions, contributed almost nothing at the
foundational layer.

China, in the decade between 2015 and 2025, built a parallel stack
covering nearly every one of these categories. It did not do so because
Chinese engineers are more talented than Indian engineers. It did so
because Chinese companies faced an existential threat that Indian
companies did not, and responded by building infrastructure.

# China\'s Full-Stack Answer {#china-stack}

The most comprehensive view of China\'s foundational OSS output is Ant
Group\'s SOFAStack. It is not a single tool --- it is an entire
infrastructure layer, built to run Alipay at scale, open-sourced under
Apache-2.0 licensing, and now commercially deployed across Chinese
financial institutions.

  Layer              Tool(s)                        Language   License
  ------------------ ------------------------------ ---------- ---------------
  Application fw     SOFABoot, SOFAArk              Java       Apache-2.0
  RPC                SOFARPC, SOFABolt              Java       Apache-2.0
  Service registry   SOFARegistry, Nacos            Java       Apache-2.0
  Distributed txn    Seata                          Java       Apache-2.0
  Messaging          RocketMQ                       Java       Apache-2.0
  Consensus          SOFAJRaft                      Java       Apache-2.0
  Service mesh       MOSN, Layotto                  Go         Apache-2.0
  Database           OceanBase, TuGraph             C++        MulanPubL-2.0
  Secure kernel      Asterinas                      Rust       MPL-2.0
  Cryptography       Tongsuo                        C          Apache-2.0
  Privacy ML         SecretFlow                     Python     Apache-2.0
  Frontend           Ant Design, AntV, Umi, Eggjs   JS/TS      MIT

Per Ant Group\'s public disclosures, SOFAStack components were adopted
by over 60% of China\'s top 50 banks. The commercialisation model is
deliberate: open the tools, close the production extensions. The LDC
router, internal registry, and risk engine that make SOFAStack
production-grade at Ant\'s scale are not public. The open-source release
is the top of an enterprise sales funnel.

The rest of China\'s tech industry follows the same pattern. Baidu\'s
PaddlePaddle is a production-grade deep learning framework with genuine
domestic enterprise deployment. BFE, Baidu\'s Go-based reverse proxy
open-sourced in 2019, handles Baidu\'s production traffic and is
technically comparable to Nginx combined with HAProxy. Huawei\'s
openEuler is a Linux distribution built as a domestic RHEL replacement;
openGauss is a PostgreSQL-derived relational database targeted at
state-owned enterprises. ByteDance\'s CloudWeGo suite --- Kitex, Hertz,
Monoio, Volo --- is Go and Rust infrastructure built to serve TikTok\'s
global traffic, with Monoio (an io_uring-based async runtime) attracting
contributors outside China.

The chart below accumulates Apache Software Foundation project donations
from Chinese companies since 2015. Each of these is a production-grade
infrastructure tool, not a research prototype.

``` {#apache-china-timeline .ojs}
apache_projects = [
  {year: 2015, project: "RocketMQ",         org: "Alibaba"},
  {year: 2017, project: "Dubbo",            org: "Alibaba"},
  {year: 2018, project: "Weex",             org: "Alibaba"},
  {year: 2019, project: "Seata",            org: "Alibaba"},
  {year: 2019, project: "Nacos",            org: "Alibaba"},
  {year: 2019, project: "ShardingSphere",   org: "JD.com"},
  {year: 2020, project: "APISIX",           org: "API7"},
  {year: 2020, project: "DolphinScheduler", org: "DiDi"},
  {year: 2021, project: "EventMesh",        org: "WeBank"},
  {year: 2021, project: "Linkis",           org: "WeBank"},
  {year: 2022, project: "Doris",            org: "Baidu"},
  {year: 2023, project: "Paimon",           org: "Alibaba"},
  {year: 2023, project: "Fury",             org: "Ant Group"},
  {year: 2024, project: "HoraeDB",          org: "Ant Group"},
]

byYear = d3.rollup(apache_projects, v => v.length, d => d.year)
yearArr = Array.from(byYear, ([year, count]) => ({year, count}))
             .sort((a, b) => a.year - b.year)
cumData = yearArr.map((d, i, arr) => ({
  year: d.year,
  cumulative: arr.slice(0, i + 1).reduce((s, x) => s + x.count, 0)
}))

Plot.plot({
  title: "Chinese infrastructure projects donated to Apache ASF",
  x: {tickFormat: "d", label: "Year"},
  y: {label: "Cumulative projects", grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.areaY(cumData, {
      x: "year", y: "cumulative",
      fill: "#d62728", fillOpacity: 0.12
    }),
    Plot.lineY(cumData, {
      x: "year", y: "cumulative",
      stroke: "#d62728", strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(cumData, {x: "year", y: "cumulative", fill: "#d62728", r: 4}),
    Plot.text(apache_projects, {
      x: "year",
      y: d => cumData.find(c => c.year === d.year)?.cumulative,
      text: "project", dy: -10, fontSize: 9, rotate: -25
    })
  ]
})
```

# The Forcing Function: IOE and the 863 Programme {#forcing-function}

None of this happened organically. China\'s infrastructure OSS output is
traceable to two forcing functions operating simultaneously: an
existential vendor dependency problem, and a government programme that
funded its replacement.

IOE --- IBM for servers, Oracle for databases, EMC for storage ---
dominated Chinese enterprise IT through the 2010s at pricing calibrated
for Western markets, with supply-chain dependencies that ran straight
through American companies. Post-2018 trade restrictions converted those
dependencies from expensive inconveniences into strategic liabilities.
The Chinese government\'s IOE replacement programme mandated domestic
alternatives at every infrastructure layer. Chinese tech companies did
not respond by writing research papers. They built software.

Ant Financial\'s OceanBase is the canonical example. Per Alibaba\'s
published accounts, Oracle\'s database could not handle Singles\' Day
transaction volumes --- the engineering problem was existential and no
amount of hardware spending could resolve it. Ant built OceanBase to
survive, not to sell. The commercial opportunity came later.

The pattern repeats at every layer: RocketMQ replaced IBM MQ. MOSN
replaced Envoy. openGauss replaced Oracle in state-owned enterprise
procurement. openEuler replaced RHEL. Every open-source release is the
downstream product of an internal survival decision made years earlier.

The government\'s role was not passive. China\'s 863 Programme --- the
National High-Technology R&D Plan, launched in 1986 --- funded specific
technology outcomes rather than broad research. It named target
capabilities and funded teams to build them. The United States ran the
same model through DARPA: TCP/IP, the internet, GPS, and the transformer
architecture underlying modern AI all originated in government
programmes with specific engineering deliverables, not open grants.
Neither China nor the US built strategic software infrastructure through
market forces alone.

# The Ecosystem Goes Deeper {#ecosystem}

The big-company view of Chinese OSS still undersells the breadth.

Gitea --- started by a Chinese developer as Gogs, now one of the most
widely self-hosted Git platforms globally --- is used by engineering
teams in Europe and North America who have no awareness of or interest
in the Chinese technology ecosystem. They use it because it is the best
tool for self-hosted version control. Forgejo, its community fork,
extended the governance model. Together they represent something Chinese
OSS rarely achieves: adoption driven entirely by technical merit, with
no geopolitical or procurement logic behind it.

Harbor, the container registry, started at VMware\'s Shanghai R&D centre
and graduated from CNCF incubation to become the de facto standard for
enterprise container registries --- deployed at organisations across
North America and Europe. TiDB, from Beijing-based PingCAP, is a
distributed SQL database competing directly with CockroachDB and
YugabyteDB on Western terms, with genuine open-source community traction
outside China.

FISCO BCOS, from WeBank (a Tencent-backed digital bank), is a genuinely
open permissioned blockchain with governance extending beyond any single
company --- a meaningful contrast with most blockchain projects that
claim openness while concentrating control.

# India\'s Genuine Contribution: The Protocol Layer {#india-dpi}

India\'s contribution to open technology is real, significant, and
operates at a completely different layer. Not code. Protocol.

The India Stack --- Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC, DigiLocker --- is a set of open
digital infrastructure specifications built on government mandate, with
private players competing on top. UPI is the clearest case: NPCI defined
the protocol and mandated zero transaction fees on peer-to-peer
payments, commoditising the payments layer and forcing every player to
compete on distribution and product. The protocol is open. The
implementations --- PhonePe, Google Pay, Paytm --- are not.

  Component   Type                 Scale (2025)       What is open
  ----------- -------------------- ------------------ ----------------------------------
  Aadhaar     Digital identity     1.3B+ enrolled     Specification; not OSS code
  UPI         Real-time payments   INR 20T+ monthly   Protocol spec; impls proprietary
  ONDC        Open commerce net    Growing            Protocol and network spec
  ABHA        Health identity      Aadhaar-linked     API specification

UPI deserves particular clarity. NPCI published the protocol and
mandated zero transaction fees on peer-to-peer payments. PhonePe, Google
Pay, and Paytm all built proprietary implementations on top of that open
specification. The protocol is open. The code is not. This is a
meaningful and replicable model --- Singapore\'s PayNow, Brazil\'s Pix,
and others drew from it --- but it is not open-source software.

This is a genuine and globally significant contribution. The UPI model
has been studied and replicated across a dozen countries. The Aadhaar
architecture influenced identity infrastructure design worldwide. But
none of it is deployable open-source code. It is foundational policy
expressed as open standards. The distinction matters when measuring who
controls the software layer.

# MOSIP: The OSS Exception That Proves the Rule {#mosip}

The Modular Open Source Identity Platform is India\'s most significant
open-source software contribution --- and it is almost unknown outside
development-sector circles. Incubated at IIIT-Bangalore in 2018, MOSIP
takes the design principles behind Aadhaar and packages them as
deployable software that any country can run without purchasing from a
Western biometric vendor.

As of 2025, MOSIP had active implementations in 14 countries, with over
100 million residents enrolled across Africa, Southeast Asia, and Latin
America. Morocco, Philippines, Ethiopia, Nigeria, Peru --- countries
building sovereign identity infrastructure on Indian-incubated software.

``` {#mosip-growth-chart .ojs}
mosip = [
  {year: 2020, residents_m: 0,   countries: 2},
  {year: 2021, residents_m: 8,   countries: 5},
  {year: 2022, residents_m: 30,  countries: 9},
  {year: 2023, residents_m: 65,  countries: 12},
  {year: 2024, residents_m: 95,  countries: 13},
  {year: 2025, residents_m: 110, countries: 14},
]

Plot.plot({
  title: "MOSIP: residents enrolled globally (millions, as of 2025)",
  x: {tickFormat: "d", label: "Year"},
  y: {label: "Residents enrolled (M)", grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.areaY(mosip, {
      x: "year", y: "residents_m",
      fill: "#1565C0", fillOpacity: 0.15
    }),
    Plot.lineY(mosip, {
      x: "year", y: "residents_m",
      stroke: "#1565C0", strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(mosip, {x: "year", y: "residents_m", fill: "#1565C0", r: 4}),
    Plot.text(mosip, {
      x: "year", y: "residents_m",
      text: d => d.countries + " countries",
      dy: -12, fontSize: 9
    }),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

MOSIP is important precisely because it is software, not just a
specification. It can be deployed, forked, improved, and adapted. In
that sense it is India\'s closest analog to what Ant did with SOFAStack.
The difference: there is no commercial tier. MOSIP is public
infrastructure with no product attached. That limits its trajectory but
also makes it a genuine public good.

# Indian Companies That Built But Did Not Open {#indian-builders}

The interesting Indian cases are not the services companies. They are
the companies that built foundationally and chose not to open-source
their work.

**Zoho** is the most striking example. Across 18 self-owned global data
centres, Zoho runs hardware and software stacks it built itself,
refusing to use hyperscalers on principle. Artos, a Linux-based
enterprise desktop OS, is under development. Ulaa, Zoho\'s own browser
launched in 2023, is built on Chromium but with Google services removed
and privacy protections added --- the same starting point as Brave,
built by a company a fraction of Zoho\'s scale.

Most significantly: Zoho built Deluge (Domain Specific Language for the
Universal Grid Environment), a proprietary scripting language that
powers the entire Zoho application stack. Every automation in Zoho CRM,
Zoho Creator, Zoho Analytics is written in Deluge. Zoho built a
programming language. That is foundational software by any definition.
None of it --- not Deluge, not Artos, not the data centre tooling ---
has been open-sourced. The depth exists. The community contribution does
not.

**PhonePe** takes a different approach: architectural transparency
without open source. The company publishes detailed technical
disclosures of its internal infrastructure. Drove, a NUMA-aware
container orchestrator. Nimbus, a bare-metal provisioning system built
on custom iPXE. PPeC Agent, managing approximately 350,000 cores and 3
petabytes of RAM across PhonePe\'s infrastructure, per their published
engineering blog. Average VM creation time: approximately seven seconds.
These are non-trivial engineering achievements. None of them is
open-source.

**Frappe** is the genuine exception. The Frappe framework --- a Python
full-stack web framework built by Frappe Technologies in Mumbai --- is
open-source and powers ERPNext, one of the most widely deployed
open-source ERP systems globally. Frappe is real OSS with a real
community. But it is application-layer software. It sits on top of
existing databases and web servers. It does not replace them. India\'s
one clear OSS product company built an application, not infrastructure.

The chart below shows where each country\'s contributions land in the
infrastructure stack.

``` {#layer-coverage-matrix .ojs}
layers = [
  "OS / Kernel", "Cryptography", "Database", "Consensus",
  "Messaging", "RPC / Networking", "Service Mesh",
  "Service Registry", "App Framework", "ML Framework",
  "Frontend / UI", "Protocol / Spec"
]

coverage = [
  {country: "China", layer: "OS / Kernel",        depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Cryptography",        depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Database",            depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Consensus",           depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Messaging",           depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "RPC / Networking",    depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Service Mesh",        depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Service Registry",    depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "App Framework",       depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "ML Framework",        depth: 2},
  {country: "China", layer: "Frontend / UI",       depth: 3},
  {country: "China", layer: "Protocol / Spec",     depth: 1},
  {country: "India", layer: "OS / Kernel",         depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Cryptography",        depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Database",            depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Consensus",           depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Messaging",           depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "RPC / Networking",    depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Service Mesh",        depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Service Registry",    depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "App Framework",       depth: 1},
  {country: "India", layer: "ML Framework",        depth: 0},
  {country: "India", layer: "Frontend / UI",       depth: 1},
  {country: "India", layer: "Protocol / Spec",     depth: 3},
]

Plot.plot({
  title: "Open-source infrastructure depth by layer (0 = none, 3 = deep)",
  marginLeft: 130,
  x: {label: null, domain: ["China", "India"]},
  y: {label: null, domain: [...layers].reverse()},
  color: {
    domain: [0, 1, 2, 3],
    range: ["#f0f0f0", "#fdae6b", "#f16913", "#7f2704"],
    legend: true,
    tickFormat: d => ["None", "Limited", "Partial", "Deep"][d]
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.cell(coverage, {
      x: "country", y: "layer",
      fill: "depth", inset: 2
    })
  ]
})
```

# The Structural Explanation {#structural}

The gap is not a talent gap. The engineers who designed AWS\'s core
infrastructure, who wrote the distributed systems that run Google and
Facebook, who built the databases that power half the internet --- many
of them trained at Indian institutions. They built foundational
software. They built it for American companies, on American payrolls,
for American users. India exported the talent and imported the
infrastructure they built.

This happened because India\'s technology industry was structured from
the beginning as a services export industry. Infosys, Wipro, TCS were
built to write code for Western clients on Western infrastructure. There
was no domestic scale problem that required building a new database.
There was no government programme that mandated replacing Oracle. The
forcing function that created Chinese infrastructure OSS simply did not
exist.

China\'s technology companies were structurally different: built for
domestic consumption at a scale that no Western SaaS company could
address. Alibaba\'s Singles\' Day traffic is not an AWS design target.
Tencent\'s WeChat user base is not a scenario that Salesforce was
architected for. When scale exceeds what purchased infrastructure can
handle, you build. When scale is someone else\'s problem, you don\'t.

The government funding dimension reinforces this. DARPA does not fund
research. It funds outcomes --- \"build a packet-switched network that
survives nuclear attack\" is an engineering contract, not a research
grant. China\'s 863 Programme operated identically: named technologies,
funded teams, specific deliverables. India\'s equivalent bodies --- DST,
SERB, NASSCOM initiatives --- fund research papers and academic
publications. Papers do not replace Oracle.

# What India Could Do {#india-path}

The standard response to India\'s foundational software gap points to
the IITs and IISc --- well-funded, internationally recognised, natural
homes for deep technical work. This is probably the wrong answer.

IIT graduates have excellent options. Google, Microsoft, and every major
US technology company recruit aggressively from the top Indian
institutions. A student at IIT Delhi who builds a distributed database
is passing up a comfortable career to do something difficult with
uncertain reward. Most do not make that choice. The incentive structure
does not reward foundational building.

The more promising candidate is the engineer at a B-grade
NAAC-accredited institution in Coimbatore or Nagpur or Bhubaneswar --- a
student with strong technical foundations, fewer premium corporate
options, and a stronger incentive to build something rather than join
something. That constraint is a forcing function. It is the same logic
that produced OceanBase: necessity, not abundance.

The missing ingredient is not talent. It is directed capital pointed at
specific engineering problems. A credible Indian infrastructure OSS
programme would look like this: ten named engineering problems --- a
distributed SQL database, a production-grade service mesh, an ML
training framework competitive with PyTorch --- funded at B-tier
institutions with engineering deliverables and timelines, not
publication targets. Not broad research grants. Engineering contracts.

The model exists. DARPA invented it. China\'s 863 Programme replicated
it. India has not tried it for software infrastructure, at any
institution, at any scale.

# Conclusion

> The software myth runs as follows: China makes hardware, India makes
> software. The reality is that China makes hardware and foundational
> software. India makes services. Services and foundational software are
> not the same thing, and the difference is not academic. The country
> that controls the database, the operating system, the RPC framework,
> and the service mesh controls the terms on which everything above
> those layers is built. China has spent a decade building that control,
> driven by a US vendor dependency it could no longer afford. India\'s
> engineers helped build the same infrastructure --- for American
> companies, for American users, on American payrolls. The talent was
> never the constraint. The forcing function was. That forcing function
> can be manufactured through policy. It has not been. Until it is, the
> gap described in this article will not close on its own.
